content.full_text:
Dee Gipson 
DRR Investments 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
P.O. Box 244 
Riverside, 
CA 
95216 
Dear Ms. Gipson: 
AdministratiOft 
322-5660 
executive/legal 
322·5901 
August 16, 1985 
Re: 
Major Donor Filing Requirement 
of Duane R. Roberts 
FPPC Advice No. A-85-133 
This is in reply to your letter dated May 23, 1985, which 
recently was referred to us by the Secretary of State's Political 
Reform Division • 
. The facts as I understand them from your letter are that Duane 
Roberts made a payment of $10,000 to purchase tickets to a campaign 
fundraiser for Governor Deukmejian. 
Mr. Roberts was to be reimbursed 
for $5,000 by other people who attended the fundraiser with him. 
In 
fact, at the time of your second inquiry to the Secretary of State, 
Mr. Roberts had received $5,000 from four people. 
You asked whether Mr. Roberts qualified as a committee under 
Gov. Code Section 82013(c) -- a "major donor committee" --
because 
of the $10,000 contribution. 
The Commission Staff has determined that when a person purchases 
tickets to a fundraising event, with the understanding that all or a 
portion of the cost of the tickets will be reimbursed by others, the 
person is not required to report the amount that will be reimbursed 
(and that amount does not count toward qualifying the person as a 
"committee"), as long as the person who purchased the tickets 
receives the reimbursement no later than the date of the fundraising 
event, or within 30 days of purchasing the tickets. 
If payment is not received within this time period, the person 
must aggregate the full cost of the fundraising tickets with all 
other contributions he or she has made to determine whether he or she 
meets the definition of a committee and must file reports. 
The 
entire payment must be reported on the campaign statement required to 
be filed, and a note should be made to explain that the filer expects 
to receive reimbursement for a portion of the costs. 
At such time as 
the person receives reimbursement, a notation must be made on the 
next campaign statement, if any, filed for that calendar year to 
reflect the reimbursement. 


Dee Gipson 
August 16, 1985 
Page 2 
In addition, it is important to note that, at the time the 
tickets are purchased, the person making the payment must inform the 
candidate or committee on whose behalf the fundraiser is held that he 
or she is acting as an intermediary for others, and must provide the 
candidate or committee with the name, address, occupation and amount 
of contribution, of each person who made a commitment to purchase any 
portion of the tickets. 
If payment is not received pursuant to the 
time period described above, the person who made the payment must 
inform the candidate or committee that the entire contribution is 
from 
the person who made the payment. 
In such a case, the candidate 
or committee must amend its campaign disclosure statement to indicate 
the person who made the payment as the contributor for the entire 
amount. 
Under the circumstances described in your letter, if Mr. Roberts 
received payment from the four people who attended the fundraiser 
with him no later than the date of the fundraising event or no later 
than 30 days after making the payment, he was not required to file a 
campaign statement as a major donor, since his own contributions did 
not total $10,000 or more. 
Please call me if you have any questions. 
JP:kt 
cc: 
Wayne Imberi 
Political Reform Division 
Secretary of State 
Sincerely, /)

Our question is--how does this affect Mr. Roberts' major donor contribution 
form? He will really only be donating $5,000 since he will be receiving 
$5,000 back from those who attended with him. 
An answer would greatly be appreciated so we wil1 know how to handle

sections.question_synthetic: Does a person who purchases campaign fundraiser tickets for $10,000 but expects to be reimbursed $5,000 by others qualify as a major donor committee under California law?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A person purchasing fundraiser tickets with expected reimbursement from others is not required to count the reimbursed amount toward major donor status if reimbursement is received by the fundraiser date or within 30 days of purchase. If reimbursement is not timely received, the full ticket cost must be reported and aggregated with other contributions to determine filing obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains original incoming request letter (dated May 23, 1985) appended to FPPC response letter (dated August 16, 1985). Also includes Form 461 (Major Donor Committee Campaign Statement). Clear Q&A structure with detailed conclusion and analysis of reimbursement timing and disclosure requirements.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether Mr. Roberts qualified as a committee under Gov. Code Section 82013(c) -- a "major donor committee" -- because of the $10,000 contribution.

CONCLUSION: The Commission Staff has determined that when a person purchases tickets to a fundraising event, with the understanding that all or a portion of the cost of the tickets will be reimbursed by others, the person is not required to report the amount that will be reimbursed (and that amount does not count toward qualifying the person as a "committee"), as long as the person who purchased the tickets receives the reimbursement no later than the date of the fundraising event, or within 30 days of purchasing the tickets. If payment is not received within this time period, the person must aggregate the full cost of the fundraising tickets with all other contributions he or she has made to determine whether he or she meets the definition of a committee and must file reports.
embedding.first_500_words: Dee Gipson DRR Investments Technical Assistance (916) 322-5662 P.O. Box 244 Riverside, CA 95216 Dear Ms. Gipson: AdministratiOft 322-5660 executive/legal 322·5901 August 16, 1985 Re: Major Donor Filing Requirement of Duane R. Roberts FPPC Advice No. A-85-133 This is in reply to your letter dated May 23, 1985, which recently was referred to us by the Secretary of State's Political Reform Division • . The facts as I understand them from your letter are that Duane Roberts made a payment of $10,000 to purchase tickets to a campaign fundraiser for Governor Deukmejian. Mr. Roberts was to be reimbursed for $5,000 by other people who attended the fundraiser with him. In fact, at the time of your second inquiry to the Secretary of State, Mr. Roberts had received $5,000 from four people. You asked whether Mr. Roberts qualified as a committee under Gov. Code Section 82013(c) -- a "major donor committee" -- because of the $10,000 contribution. The Commission Staff has determined that when a person purchases tickets to a fundraising event, with the understanding that all or a portion of the cost of the tickets will be reimbursed by others, the person is not required to report the amount that will be reimbursed (and that amount does not count toward qualifying the person as a "committee"), as long as the person who purchased the tickets receives the reimbursement no later than the date of the fundraising event, or within 30 days of purchasing the tickets. If payment is not received within this time period, the person must aggregate the full cost of the fundraising tickets with all other contributions he or she has made to determine whether he or she meets the definition of a committee and must file reports. The entire payment must be reported on the campaign statement required to be filed, and a note should be made to explain that the filer expects to receive reimbursement for a portion of the costs. At such time as the person receives reimbursement, a notation must be made on the next campaign statement, if any, filed for that calendar year to reflect the reimbursement. Dee Gipson August 16, 1985 Page 2 In addition, it is important to note that, at the time the tickets are purchased, the person making the payment must inform the candidate or committee on whose behalf the fundraiser is held that he or she is acting as an intermediary for others, and must provide the candidate or committee with the name, address, occupation and amount of contribution, of each person who made a commitment to purchase any portion of the tickets. If payment is not received pursuant to the time period described above, the person who made the payment must inform the candidate or committee that the entire contribution is from the person who made the payment. In such a case, the candidate or committee must amend its campaign disclosure statement to indicate the person who made the payment as the contributor for the entire amount.
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether Duane Roberts' $10,000 campaign fundraiser ticket purchase, with expected $5,000 reimbursement from attendees, qualifies him as a major donor committee. The Commission determined that timely reimbursements need not be counted toward major donor status, but established detailed disclosure requirements and notification procedures for intermediary ticket purchases.
